behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the handoff between real git rename detection and CLI categorization. A unit test over parser/counting logic can miss real rename semantics (e.g., similarity-threshold rename detection and status formats) produced by actual git commands. This behavior depends on real subprocess + repository state, so System is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp git repo, commit baseline, then `git mv` a file (and optionally content-edit to vary similarity). Run the CLI via subprocess against that repo and assert report shows the file only in `renamed`, with no corresponding `added`/`deleted` entries.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is a real I/O/subprocess/encoding boundary: binary diffs and git output handling can fail only with real files and real git process behavior. Mocked diff text can hide decode/exit-code/path handling failures.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp git repo, add/modify a binary file and a text file, then run the CLI as a subprocess. Assert process exits successfully and report includes binary-file changes in counts without traceback/crash.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The failure commonly comes from real git behavior on unborn HEAD (command exit codes/messages). In-process fakes may not reproduce that exact failure boundary. Requires real repository + subprocess interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repo with no commits, invoke the CLI as a subprocess, and assert exit code is success plus an empty structured report on stdout (not an error/traceback).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness claim against actual repository history, which depends on real git history traversal and subprocess/file interactions. Lower-level tests can validate counting logic but can miss real history extraction mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real repo history with known added/deleted/renamed/modified events across several commits. Run CLI subprocess for the target range and compare reported category counts to expected counts derived from the scripted commit sequence.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Requires real git index vs working-tree semantics (staged/unstaged) and likely distinct git command modes. This is an OS/filesystem/subprocess interaction boundary; mocks can mask index-state behavior differences.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, create equivalent change sets once as unstaged and once staged. Run the CLI subprocess in both states and assert the structured stdout report is byte-for-byte identical (or canonicalized-object identical).
